refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates three distinct obligations: (1) deriving requirements from context, (2) recording them, and (3) managing them over time. Derivation is the primary functional obligation in the problem domain; recording and managing are implementation concerns that belong in the DP/IM layers. Splitting these into atomic FRs allows independent verification and clearer traceability to design decisions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that given a worksite mission context (e.g., site layout, production targets, safety constraints), the system produces a documented set of performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) that are traceable to input parameters and stakeholder requirements.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-005.1: The NGHE solution shall derive performance targets (cycle time, energy efficiency, availability) from worksite mission inputs.
FR-NGHE-005.2: The NGHE solution shall record derived performance targets in a retrievable format.
FR-NGHE-005.3: The NGHE solution shall maintain a history of performance target baselines and calibration updates.
FR-NGHE-005.4: The NGHE solution shall establish traceability between worksite inputs and derived performance targets.

candidate_dps: DP1: Capture worksite mission context (operational objectives, environmental constraints, safety policies) via structured intake process; parse into a normalized context model; apply context-to-performance mapping rules to derive candidate performance targets; validate targets against worksite constraints; document traceability chain from context inputs to each target.

DP2: Maintain a centralized performance baseline registry indexed by worksite context fingerprint; on each mission intake, query registry for historical baselines matching current context; apply delta-analysis to detect context drift; recompute targets only if drift exceeds threshold; otherwise reuse validated baseline with audit trail.

DP3: Decompose worksite mission into functional sub-domains (safety, productivity, environmental); assign performance targets independently per sub-domain using domain-specific mapping rules; aggregate targets via weighted composition function; document sub-domain ownership and rule provenance.

DP4: Implement a two-stage derivation pipeline: (Stage 1) Extract raw mission parameters from worksite inputs and normalize to canonical form; (Stage 2) Apply inference engine (rule-based or model-based) to map normalized parameters to performance targets; maintain separation of data ingestion from logic to enable independent evolution of each stage.

DP5: Establish a versioned performance target schema with explicit fields for: derived value, source context, derivation timestamp, confidence level, and linked calibration/baseline version; store all historical versions; enable rollback and audit queries; provide API for downstream systems to query current targets with full provenance metadata.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4 + DP5 (integrated): Implement a two-stage derivation pipeline (normalization → inference) with versioned, fully-traceable performance target schema. Stage 1 decouples worksite input parsing from derivation logic. Stage 2 applies context-to-performance mapping via a pluggable inference layer. All targets are stored with full provenance (source context, timestamp, confidence, linked baseline version) and versioned for audit and rollback. This design supports independent evolution of input handling, mapping rules, and schema while maintaining traceability and enabling historical baseline management.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between input normalization and inference rules: mitigated by explicit Stage 1/Stage 2 boundary and canonical context model.
Inference rules hardcoded to specific worksite types: mitigated by parameterized rule engine and versioned schema allowing rule updates without code changes.
Performance target schema tightly coupled to downstream consumers: mitigated by explicit provenance metadata fields and API contract; downstream systems query via stable interface.
Baseline registry keyed only by context fingerprint: risk if context representation is incomplete; mitigated by explicit confidence field and delta-analysis threshold to flag uncertain matches.
coupling_check.notes: DP4+DP5 achieves low coupling by separating concerns (input parsing, inference, storage/versioning) and using explicit interfaces (canonical context model, versioned schema, provenance metadata). Inference rules remain decoupled from specific worksite implementations via parameterization. Historical baseline management is supported without requiring re-derivation of all targets on each mission.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite mission context can be normalized into a canonical form that is sufficient for performance target derivation. RISK: If context is highly heterogeneous or domain-specific, normalization may lose critical information. MITIGATION: Include confidence field in schema; flag low-confidence derivations for manual review.

ASSUMPTION: Context-to-performance mapping rules are stable and can be versioned independently of target schema. RISK: If rules are tightly coupled to specific worksite policies, versioning becomes complex. MITIGATION: Establish rule governance process; version rules separately from schema; maintain rule provenance in target metadata.

ASSUMPTION: Historical baselines are reusable across similar mission contexts. RISK: If worksite conditions drift significantly, reused baselines may become invalid. MITIGATION: Implement delta-analysis threshold; require explicit validation before reuse; log all baseline reuse decisions.

ASSUMPTION: Downstream systems can consume performance targets via API with full provenance metadata. RISK: If consumers require different metadata or formats, integration becomes complex. MITIGATION: Design schema with extensible provenance fields; provide multiple query/export formats; establish consumer feedback loop.

ASSUMPTION: Calibration/baseline updates are infrequent and can be tracked as discrete events. RISK: If calibration is continuous or event-driven, audit trail may become unwieldy. MITIGATION: Implement time-windowed aggregation for audit logs; provide query filters by date range and update type.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: 'The NGHE solution shall derive performance requirements from worksite mission context via a two-stage pipeline (normalization and inference), maintain a versioned registry of performance targets with full provenance (source context, derivation timestamp, confidence, linked baseline), support historical baseline reuse with delta-analysis validation, and provide an audit trail of all target derivations and calibration updates.' This revision clarifies the DP boundary (two-stage pipeline + versioned schema) and makes acceptance criteria testable: (1) all targets traceable to worksite inputs, (2) provenance metadata complete and queryable, (3) baseline history maintained with rollback capability, (4) audit trail captures all derivation and calibration events.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized performance target derivation pipeline with versioned schema, provenance tracking, and audit-trail management. Owns the two-stage normalization→inference architecture, target versioning, and historical baseline management.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives worksite context, sensor data, and operational parameters from Vehicle and Teleoperation for Stage 1 normalization and input parsing.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outputs derived performance targets with full provenance (source context, timestamp, confidence, linked baseline version) to Vehicle and Teleoperation for execution.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety constraints and context from Safety Services; outputs confidence-adjusted targets and audit records for safety-critical baseline rollback decisions.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the logical home for derivation logic, schema versioning, and provenance tracking. The two-stage pipeline (normalization + inference) aligns with NGHE's role as a decision/optimization layer. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry input and Tasking output cross boundaries; versioning and audit trails remain internal. Risk: if NGHE is primarily a real-time inference engine, the audit/versioning overhead may require architectural extension.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a physical/execution platform, not a derivation engine. Placing the pipeline here couples target generation to vehicle-specific hardware, violates separation of concerns, and forces duplication if multiple vehicles or teleoperation modes exist. High diffusion: normalization logic, inference rules, and versioning would leak into vehicle control logic.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a command/control interface, not a derivation engine. Placing the pipeline here couples target generation to operator interaction patterns, making it difficult to reuse derivation logic for autonomous or scheduled tasks. High diffusion: schema versioning and audit trails would be scattered across operator UI and backend logic.

Weak fit. Safety Services should validate and constrain targets, not generate them. Placing the full pipeline here conflates derivation with safety enforcement, reducing modularity and making it harder to evolve mapping rules independently of safety policies. Moderate diffusion: performance inference logic would be entangled with safety constraint checking.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the most cohesive boundary for the integrated DP4+DP5 pipeline. It naturally owns the two-stage derivation logic (normalization and inference), versioned schema storage, and provenance tracking. External dependencies are minimal and well-defined: Telemetry feeds input, Tasking distributes outputs, and SafetyAlert provides constraint context. The module can evolve its inference rules, schema versions, and audit mechanisms independently without forcing changes to Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services.
containment_risks: If NGHE Solution is currently designed as a stateless, real-time inference engine, adding versioned schema storage and full provenance tracking may require architectural extension (e.g., persistent store, audit log service). Verify that NGHE can accommodate state management and historical queries.

Telemetry interface must be rich enough to convey full worksite context (not just sensor readings) to enable Stage 1 normalization. If Telemetry is currently a thin, low-level data stream, it may need enrichment or a separate context channel.

SafetyAlert interface must support bidirectional communication: Safety Services must be able to query target provenance and confidence for rollback decisions, and NGHE must be able to request safety constraint updates. Verify that SafetyAlert supports this coupling.

If multiple independent derivation pipelines are needed (e.g., for different vehicle types or operational modes), NGHE ownership may create a bottleneck. Consider whether NGHE should own a shared framework that other modules instantiate.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. NGHE Solution is the correct boundary. However, recommend clarifying NGHE's architectural scope to explicitly include: (1) a versioned schema registry with rollback capability, (2) a provenance/audit log store, and (3) a pluggable inference layer interface. These are logical extensions of a derivation engine and do not require DP reformulation. If NGHE is currently too lightweight to support these, consider proposing a new DP that decomposes versioning and audit concerns into a separate 'Performance Target Registry' module, with NGHE owning only the derivation logic and delegating persistence to the registry via a well-defined interface.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4+DP5 (two-stage normalization→inference pipeline with versioned, fully-traceable schema) directly addresses FR requirement to derive performance requirements from worksite mission context. The integrated design explicitly decouples input parsing (Stage 1) from context-to-performance mapping (Stage 2), enabling independent evolution. Versioned schema with full provenance (source context, timestamp, confidence, linked baseline version) satisfies traceability and audit requirements. Design is logically sound and complete.

diffusion_risks: Versioning and audit-trail management (provenance tracking, historical baseline storage, rollback capability) are currently described as internal to NGHE Solution but lack explicit architectural ownership. Risk: if NGHE delegates persistence to an external store without a well-defined interface contract, versioning logic may diffuse across NGHE and the persistence layer, creating tight coupling and complicating independent evolution of schema or audit policies.

SafetyAlert interface is bidirectional (inout) but lacks explicit protocol for confidence-adjusted target queries and rollback decision support. Risk: if Safety Services must directly inspect NGHE's internal provenance store to make rollback decisions, this creates hidden coupling and potential diffusion of audit-trail interpretation logic into Safety Services.

Telemetry input interface must convey full worksite context (not just sensor telemetry) to enable Stage 1 normalization. Risk: if Telemetry is currently a thin, low-level data stream, NGHE may need to implement context enrichment logic that should logically belong to a separate context aggregation layer, causing diffusion of Stage 1 responsibilities.

Pluggable inference layer (Stage 2) is described as extensible but lacks explicit module boundary for inference rule registration, versioning, and lifecycle management. Risk: if inference rules are embedded directly in NGHE code rather than stored in a versioned registry, adding new rules or updating existing ones will require NGHE redeployment, violating the independence principle.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary (containment score 0.92). The two-stage pipeline, versioned schema, and provenance tracking are logically cohesive and align with NGHE's role as a decision/optimization layer. External interfaces (Telemetry in, Tasking out, SafetyAlert inout) are well-defined and minimal. However, three architectural clarifications are required to minimize diffusion: (1) Explicit versioned schema registry with rollback API (owned by NGHE or delegated via contract), (2) Pluggable inference layer with rule versioning and lifecycle management, (3) Provenance/audit log interface that Safety Services can query without direct access to internal state. These clarifications do not change DP ownership but strengthen the IM boundary definition.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria require 'documented set of performance targets traceable to worksite inputs' and 'history of baselines/calibration updates.' NGHE Solution's ownership of versioned schema, full provenance tracking (source context, timestamp, confidence, linked baseline version), and audit-trail management directly enables verification. Traceability can be validated by querying provenance records; baseline history can be audited via version control. Verification is feasible if NGHE exposes provenance and versioning APIs for inspection.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Good, with one clarification needed. FR allocation to NGHE Solution is clear: derive performance targets from worksite context via two-stage pipeline. However, the allocation of 'worksite safety plan and operational policies' as input constraints is implicit. Recommend explicit clarification: (1) Are safety plans and policies inputs to Stage 1 (normalization) or Stage 2 (inference)? (2) Does NGHE own policy interpretation, or does it receive pre-processed constraints from Safety Services? Current SafetyAlert interface suggests Safety Services provides constraints, but the exact handoff point is ambiguous. Clarify whether NGHE receives raw policies or validated constraint sets.

recommended_revisions: Extend NGHE Solution's architectural scope to explicitly include: (a) a versioned performance target schema registry with rollback capability, (b) a provenance/audit log store with query API, and (c) a pluggable inference layer interface with rule versioning. These are logical extensions of the derivation engine and do not require DP reformulation.

Define a formal contract for the SafetyAlert interface: (1) Safety Services queries NGHE for target provenance and confidence to support rollback decisions, (2) NGHE requests safety constraint updates from Safety Services, (3) both parties agree on schema for constraint representation and confidence thresholds. This prevents diffusion of audit-trail interpretation logic into Safety Services.

Clarify Telemetry interface: specify whether it conveys full worksite context (mission objectives, environmental conditions, operational policies) or only low-level sensor data. If only sensor data, propose a separate 'Context Aggregation' input channel or require Telemetry to be enriched upstream. This prevents Stage 1 normalization logic from diffusing into context assembly.

Formalize the pluggable inference layer: define a rule registration API, versioning scheme, and lifecycle management (e.g., rule activation, deprecation, rollback). Store inference rules in a versioned registry (owned by NGHE or delegated via contract) rather than embedding them in code. This prevents diffusion of rule evolution logic into deployment or configuration management.

Add explicit acceptance criterion: 'NGHE Solution shall expose provenance and versioning APIs enabling external audit of target derivation history and baseline rollback without direct access to internal state.' This operationalizes traceability and prevents Safety Services or other modules from bypassing NGHE's audit controls.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass logically, confirming that the two-stage normalization→inference pipeline with versioned schema is sound and traceable. However, the DP-IM gate identifies four critical diffusion risks and the FR-IM gate reveals an allocation ambiguity regarding policy interpretation ownership. Given high safety criticality, these architectural clarifications must be resolved at the current level before decomposing further. Proceeding to sub-FRs without resolving IM boundary definition and interface contracts risks propagating ambiguity into lower levels, complicating verification and creating hidden coupling. REVISE_CURRENT_LEVEL is the appropriate decision: strengthen DP and IM definitions to eliminate diffusion risks and clarify policy constraint handling, then re-gate before decomposition.

revise.why: Four specific revisions are required to eliminate diffusion risks and clarify architectural boundaries: (1) Extend DP4+DP5 to explicitly include a versioned performance target schema registry with rollback API, owned by NGHE Solution or delegated via formal contract. This prevents versioning logic from diffusing into external persistence layers. (2) Define a pluggable inference layer interface (part of DP5) with explicit rule registration, versioning, and lifecycle management APIs. Store inference rules in a versioned registry rather than embedding in code. This prevents rule evolution logic from diffusing into deployment or configuration management. (3) Formalize the SafetyAlert interface contract: specify that Safety Services queries NGHE for target provenance and confidence to support rollback decisions, and NGHE requests validated constraint sets from Safety Services. Define schema for constraint representation and confidence thresholds. This prevents audit-trail interpretation logic from diffusing into Safety Services. (4) Clarify Telemetry input interface: explicitly specify whether it conveys full worksite context (mission objectives, environmental conditions, operational policies) or only low-level sensor data. If only sensor data, propose a separate 'Context Aggregation' input channel or require upstream enrichment. This prevents Stage 1 normalization logic from diffusing into context assembly. Additionally, clarify FR allocation: explicitly state whether safety plans and policies are inputs to Stage 1 (normalization) or Stage 2 (inference), and whether NGHE owns policy interpretation or receives pre-processed constraints from Safety Services.

assumptions: NGHE Solution is the correct ownership boundary for the two-stage derivation pipeline (containment score 0.92 confirmed).
Versioned schema, provenance tracking, and audit-trail management are logically cohesive and belong within NGHE's scope.
Safety Services is a separate system responsible for safety constraint validation and rollback decision support.
Telemetry is an external input source; the extent to which it conveys context (vs. raw sensor data) is currently ambiguous.
Inference rules are extensible and pluggable, but the mechanism for rule registration, versioning, and lifecycle management is not yet formalized.
High safety criticality requires explicit interface contracts and clear ownership boundaries to prevent hidden coupling and enable independent evolution.
open_risks: Versioning and audit-trail management may diffuse into external persistence layers if NGHE delegates storage without a well-defined interface contract. Mitigation: define explicit versioned schema registry API owned by NGHE or delegated via formal SLA.

SafetyAlert interface lacks explicit protocol for confidence-adjusted target queries and rollback decision support. Risk: Safety Services may directly inspect NGHE's internal provenance store, creating hidden coupling. Mitigation: formalize SafetyAlert contract with explicit query/response schemas.

Telemetry input may be insufficient to enable Stage 1 normalization if it conveys only low-level sensor data without worksite context. Risk: NGHE may implement context enrichment logic that should belong to a separate layer. Mitigation: clarify Telemetry interface or propose separate Context Aggregation input.

Pluggable inference layer lacks explicit module boundary for rule registration and versioning. Risk: inference rules embedded in code require NGHE redeployment for updates, violating independence principle. Mitigation: formalize rule registration API and versioned registry.

Policy constraint handling allocation is ambiguous: unclear whether NGHE owns policy interpretation or receives pre-processed constraints from Safety Services. Risk: diffusion of policy logic across NGHE and Safety Services. Mitigation: explicitly clarify policy input ownership and handoff point.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of versioned schema registry API: schema versioning scheme, rollback capability, query interface, and ownership (NGHE or delegated).

Formal specification of pluggable inference layer interface: rule registration API, versioning scheme, lifecycle management (activation, deprecation, rollback), and storage mechanism (versioned registry vs. embedded code).

Formal SafetyAlert interface contract: query/response schemas for target provenance, confidence levels, rollback decision support, and constraint updates. Specify whether Safety Services queries NGHE directly or via a well-defined API.

Clarification of Telemetry input interface: explicit specification of data elements conveyed (sensor telemetry only vs. full worksite context including mission objectives, environmental conditions, operational policies). If context is not included, propose separate Context Aggregation input or upstream enrichment mechanism.

Clarification of policy constraint handling: explicit statement of whether safety plans and policies are inputs to Stage 1 (normalization) or Stage 2 (inference), and whether NGHE owns policy interpretation or receives pre-processed constraints from Safety Services.

Acceptance criterion for provenance and versioning APIs: 'NGHE Solution shall expose provenance and versioning APIs enabling external audit of target derivation history and baseline rollback without direct access to internal state.' Verification method: API specification and audit trail query examples.
